RAy! MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
September 4, 1997
Kevin Ennis
Richards, Watson & Gershon
333 South Hope Street, 38th Floor
Los Angeles, California 90071-1469
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-289
Dear Mr. Ennis:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalfof Councilmember Sally
Flowers about the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan?
2. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to select consultants
to gather additional information and to revise the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan?
3. (a) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to designate a new
proposed redevelopment project area boundary without simultaneously adopting a
redevelopment plan for the new area?
3. (b) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt a new
redevelopment plan for a new proposed project area?
4. (a) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place a non
binding question before the voters for their approval or disapproval of redevelopment in general?
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-289
Page 2
4. (b) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place a non
binding question before the voters for their approval or disapproval of the 1996 proposed
redevelopment project area and the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan?
5. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to designate a
governing body, other than the members of the Council, to act as the Board of the Agency?
6. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt an ordinance
deactivating the Agency?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest in the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan arising from her residence, and from her father and aunt who are sources of
income to her. She may have a conflict of interest arising from the operation of the “personal
effects” rule. Whether she is disqualified as a result ofthese conflicts depends on whether the
public generally exception applies. We cannot make this determination from a distance. We
have provided the relevant legal analysis, and identified the critical questions of fact Ms. Flowers
must answer.
2. The decision to select consultants to gather information and to revise the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan and the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan are too
closely related to be considered separately. Thus, ifMs. Flowers is disqualified with regard to
the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified with regard
to the decision to select consultants to gather information and to revise the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan.
3. (a) and 3. (b). We cannot answer these questions because they are entirely hypothetical.
4. (a) We cannot answer this question because it is entirely hypothetical.
4. (b) If Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to the decision to adopt
the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified from taking part in the decision to
place the matter on the ballot.
5.
The decision to designate an alternative governing body for the Agency and the decision to
adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan are too closely related to be considered separately.
Thus, ifMs. Flowers is disqualified with regard to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified with regard to the decision to designate an
alternative governing body to act as the Board of the Agency.


File No. A-97-289
Page 3
6. The decision to deactivate the Agency and the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan are too closely related to be considered separately. Thus, if Ms. Flowers is
disqualified with regard to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is
also disqualified with regard to the decision to deactivate the Agency.
FACTS
You are the deputy city attorney for the City of Artesia (“City”) and have been authorized
by Ms. Flowers, a member ofthe Artesia City Council (“Council”) to seek formal advice.
In 1995, the City activated a Redevelopment Agency (“Agency”). The Council
designated itself to sit as the governing board (“Board”) of the Agency. In 1996, a proposed
project area (“1996 proposed project area”) was prepared by staff and consultants. The 1996
proposed project area included all commercially zoned property in the City, and excluded all
residentially zoned property in the City.
The 1996 proposed project area and the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan were
presented to the Council in mid-1996. During the Council’s consideration ofthose documents,
the general subject of redevelopment became an issue of significant community debate. Finally,
in August 1996, the Council decided not to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
In order to gain community consensus, the Council then considered whether to seek the
views ofthe voters in Artesia by way of a vote regarding redevelopment. In exploring that
option, the city attorney’s office advised the Council that it has the power to place a non-binding
initiative on the ballot on whether to adopt the 1996 proposed project area, or whether to pursue
redevelopment generally. Although the measure would not legally bind the Council, it appears
unlikely as a political matter that the Council would vote to pursue redevelopment of any sort in
the near future if the electors voted against the concept.
As an alternative to seeking voter approval, and based on the recent election of two new
members to the Council, the Council wants to consider: (i) whether to designate a governing
body, other than the members of the Council, to act as the Board of the Agency; (ii) whether to
select consultants to gather additional information; (iii) whether to revise the 1996 proposed
project area and the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan; (iv) whether to designate a new
hypothetical project area boundary without an implementing plan; (v) whether to adopt a
redevelopment plan for that area; and (vi) whether to adopt an ordinance deactivating the
Agency.
Under the redevelopment law, if the Council decides to move forward with the adoption
of a new preliminary plan, new proceedings would have to occur and the documents would have
to be updated and revised.


File No. A-97-289
Page 4
Ms. Sally Flowers serves on the Council. Ms. Flowers resides in, and is the sole owner
of, a single-family residence located approximately 300 feet from the boundaries of the 1996
proposed project area. The City’s Planning Director has determined that more than 10 percent of
all homeowners in the City are located within 300 feet from the proposed boundaries of the 1996
project area.
Ms. Flowers has provided weekly cleaning services of the common areas of a building
owned by her father and aunt. For that service, Ms. Flowers has received approximately $25 per
week from her father. Ms. Flowers discontinued these services at the end ofMarch 1997.
During the last twelve months, she has received more than $250 from her father for the service.
Ms. Flowers’ father and aunt own an office building located on a lot within the 1996
proposed project area. Ms. Flowers’ father also owns an interest in a commercially zoned
property located within the 1996 proposed project area. Ms. Flowers’ father operates a trailer
rental yard on the second property.
Ms. Flowers has a roommate. The roommate does not pay Ms. Flowers rent, but does
contribute to the payment of shared household expenses. The contribution to shared expenses
are estimated by Ms. Flowers to have exceeded $250 in the last twelve months.
The roommate has been employed by, and has received more than $250 in income in the
last twelve months from Russell Construction. Russell Construction provides temporary laborers
and contractors to contractors working on projects throughout southern California.
For more than the last twelve months, Ms. Flowers’ roommate has been assigned by
Russell Construction to woi* almost exclusively onjob sites for a contractor called Snyder
Langston. Snyder Langston has not performed any contracting work in the City of Artesia
during the last twelve months and it is not anticipated that it will work on a project in Artesia in
the future.
Snyder Langston pays Russell Construction for the contractors and laborers it provides on
Snyder Langston’s job sites, including Ms. Flowers’ roommate. Russell Construction then pays
Ms. Flowers’ roommate for his services.
When Ms. Flowers’ roommate was not working on ajob site for Snyder Langston, he
worked through Russell Construction for other contractors, none ofwhose job sites were within
the City of Artesia. Thus, Ms. Flowers’ roommate has not worked on job sites in Artesia and it is
not anticipated that he will do so in the future. Ms. Flowers’ roommate recently became
temporarily disabled by a work-related injury and may or may not return to work for Russell
Construction.


File No. A-97-289
Page 5
Snyder Langston does not own property or operate a business in the City. Russell
Construction leases office space in the City of Artesia in a building that is located within the
boundaries of the originally proposed project area. Russell Construction is a company that is
smaller in financial size than the criteria specified in Section 18702.2(f) for listing on the
NASDAQ.
ANALYSIS
A.
Introduction to the conflict-of-interest provisions.
The purpose of the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that public officials
will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. As a public official,
2 Ms. Flowers will have a disqualifying
conflict of interest with regard to governmental decisions about the redevelopment project in the
City of Artesia if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on
her financial interests which are distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
B.
Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where the public official “make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.”
(Section 87100.)
By voting on any of the potential Council decisions about which you inquire,
Ms. Flowers would be making governmental decisions. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(1).) By taking
part in deliberations and negotiations leading up to a vote, she would be using her official
position to influence the decisions. (Regulation 18700.1(a).)
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are designed to be applied on a decision-by-
decision basis. Each of the various Council decisions about which you have inquired must be
addressed separately.
2 The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to “public officials.” “Public official,” for purposes
of the Act, is defmed to include every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local agency (with
certain exceptions not relevant here). (Section 82048; Regulation 87100.) As a member of the City Council,
Ms. Flowers is a public official.


File No. A-97-289
Page 6
C.
Identifying financial interests.
1. Introduction.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only tofinancial conflicts. “Financial
interest” is defined in Section 87103. Section 87103 recognizes six kinds of financial interests
for purposes of the Act:
•
A business entity in which the public official has an investment of $1,000 or more;
•
Real property in which the public official has an interest of $1,000 or more;
•
Any source of income which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the
governmental decision;
•
A business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.;
•
A donor of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $290 or more within 12
months prior to the decision;
•
Finally, the public official has a fmancial interest if the governmental decision will have a
“personal effect” on himlher or his/her immediate family, whether positive or negative, of
at least $250 in any 12-month period. (This is known as the “personal effects” rule.)
(Section 87103; Regulation rl 8702.1 (a)(4).)
2. Real property interest
Presuming that Ms. Flowers has invested more than $1,000, she has a financial interest in
her residence. (Section 87103(b).)
3. Source of income interests
(a) Herfather and aunt
Because she has received more than $250 in income in the past twelve months from her
father and aunt, they are a source of income to Ms. Flowers. (Section 87103(c).)


File No. A-97-289
Page 7
(b) Her roommate
Ms. Flowers’ roommate periodically pays for Ms. Flowers’ dinners and groceries, and
also pays for the utilities. The utilities payment is not a gift because Ms. Flowers provides
consideration of at least equal value to the roommate in the form ofhousing. (Section 82028.)
However, the payments are income to Ms. Flowers, and the roommate is that source of income.
(Section 82030(a); Ball Advice Letter, No. 97-192.) Therefore, the roommate is a source of
income to Ms. Flowers. (Section 87103(c); Ball Advice Letter, supra.)
4. Personal effects rule.
Ms. Flowers must consider whether each ofthe potential decisions about which you have
inquired will have a personal financial effect on her or on her immediate family.
3 (Section
87103, first paragraph.) She may have a financial interest in a given decision if the reasonably
foreseeable impact of the decision results in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities
ofher or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at least $250 in any 12-month period.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)
D. Reasonable foreseeability and materiality.
Whether the financial consequences of a given governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific. A financial effect need
not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will
occur suffices to meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Whether a financial effect is material is determined under various regulations
promulgated by the FPPC, depending upon the nature of the interest and the degree to which it is
involved. (Regulation 18700 et seq.)
It is important to understand that determinations of reasonable foreseeability and
materiality are very fact-dependent, and must be made on a decision-by-decision basis. An effect
which may not be reasonably foreseeable at an early stage of a process may become reasonably
foreseeable as the process unfolds. A “blanket” determination of reasonable foreseeability
cannot be made at any stage of a process or a series of decisions which applies to the entire
process or series of decisions. Therefore, the reasonable foreseeability and materiality of the
potential governmental decisions about which you inquire on Ms. Flowers’ various financial
interests must be considered separately.
For purposes of the Act, “immediate family” means the spouse and dependent children. (Section
82029.)


File No. A-97-289
Page 8
1. A decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
(a) Realproperty interest
Ms. Flowers has a financial interest in the residence she owns which would be indirectly
involved in a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
4 Whether the financial
effect of a given governmental decision on an indirectly involved real property interest is
material is determined under Regulation 18702.3. That regulation prescribes alternative
materiality rules; which one applies depends primarily upon the geographic proximity ofthe
interest to the area which is the subject ofthe decision.
As to reasonable foreseeability, it is reasonably foreseeable that at least some financial
effect on real property values and business interests located within or near project areas will
result from redevelopment projects. (DeSaulnier Advice Letter, No. A-96-361.) A financial
effect on Ms. Flowers’ real property interest is thus reasonably foreseeable because it is located
near the 1996 proposed project boundaries. The critical issue is thus whether this effect would
be material.
Ms. Flowers’ residence is within 300 feet of the 1996 proposed project area boundaries.
A real property interest within 300 feet ofthe area which is the subject of a decision is deemed to
be materially affected by the decision, unless it can be shown that no financial effect will result.
(Regulation 18702.3(a)(l).) This is a “one penny” rule: if there is any financial effect
whatsoever, Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest. As it is difficult to imagine that there will be
no financial effect whatsoever on Ms. Flowers’ residence, she has a conflict of interest, and is
disqualified unless the “public generally exception” applies. (See part IV.E, below.)
(b) Source ofincome interests
(1) Herfather and aunt
Her father and aunt are a source of income to Ms. Flowers. (See part IV.C, above.) They
are directly involved in a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan because they
own commercial buildings and business entities located within the 1996 proposed project
boundaries. (Regulation 18702.l(b)(2).) A financial effect on Ms. Flowers’ father and aunt and
their real property and business interests is reasonably foreseeable because the property and
businesses are located within the 1996 proposed project area. (DeSaulnier, supra.) Any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a directly involved source of income is deemed to be
material. (Regulation 18702.1 (a)(l).) Therefore, Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest arising
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(3) states the conditions in which a real property interest is considered to be
directly involved in a governmental decision. None of these conditions are true as to Ms. Flowers’ real property
interest in comiection with a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan. Therefore, by default, the
interest would be indirectly involved in the decision.


File No. A-97-289
Page 9
from her father and aunt as a source of income, and is disqualified unless the public generally
exception applies. (See below.)
(ii) Her roommate
Ms. Flowers’ roommate, who is a source of income to her, is indirectly involved in the
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan. Although he is employed in the
construction trades, and it is likely that a redevelopment decision will have some impact on the
local construction trades, it would be speculative at best to try to gauge the impact ofthe decision
on a particular employee. Therefore, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to adopt
the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan will have a financial effect, much less a material one, on
Ms. Flowers’ roommate. She would not have a conflict of interest based on her roommate as a
source of income.
(c) Personal effects rule
When applying the personal effects rule, financial effects on the value ofreal property
owned by the public official are discounted. (Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4).) Such financial effects
are analyzed independently, as we have done above. Therefore, the effects of the decision on her
residence are discounted when applying the personal effects rule to Ms. Flowers. Discounting
the effects on her residence, Ms. Flowers must decide if a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan will have a “personal effect” on her or her immediate family, whether
positive or negative, of at least $250 in any 12-month period. If so, she has a conflict of interest,
and is disqualified unless the public generally exception applies.
2. A decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan.
The answer to question no. 2 depends largely on the answer to question no. 1: if
Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, then she will also have a disqualifying conflict of interest with
regard to a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan. The two decisions are inextricably interrelated. To state only the
most obvious evidence oftheir interrelatedness, a decision to select consultants to gather
additional information and to revise the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan amounts to a
decision not to adopt the original 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
Under certain circumstances, a complex decision may be divided into a series of separate
decisions if an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in one of the component-decisions
which is not interdependent upon other components. (Cook Advice Letter, No. 1-91-468.)
However, a series of decisions may be too interrelated to be considered separately.
In the Cook
Advice Letter, supra, we advised that this would be true where a decision on one aspect of a
general plan will substantially affect a decision on another aspect of the plan.


File No. A-97-289
Page 10
Here, the relationship between a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan
and a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan are inextricably interrelated. Therefore, if you conclude that
Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, then she will also have a disqualifying conflict of interest with
regard to a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan.
Even if you conclude that Ms. Flowers is not disqualified with regard to a decision to
adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she may nonetheless have a conflict of interest
with regard to a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the
1996 proposed redevelopment plan. We are unable to advise you on this particular issue because
it is hypothetical.
(Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(D).) The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are
designed to be applied in the context of a particular governmental decision. For example,
Ms. Flowers might have a conflict of interest with regard to one consultant because that
consultant is a source of income to her, while she would not have a conflict of interest with
regard to another consultant with whom she has no financial relationship. Similarly, we cannot
advise about revisions to the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan without knowing at least some
of the details of the revision and the expected impact on Ms. Flowers’ interests.
3. (a) A decision to designate anew proposed redevelopment project area boundary
without simultaneously adopting a redevelopment plan for the new area.
3. (b) A decision to adopt a new redevelopment plan for a new proposed project are&
We cannot answer these questions because each is hypothetical. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(D).) The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are designed to be applied in the
context of a particular decision. For example, we cannot advise you about a hypothetical
decision to designate new proposed project boundaries without knowing the geographical
relationship of the boundaries to Ms. Flowers’ real property and other financial interests. We
cannot advise about a decision to adopt a redevelopment plan for this hypothetical new project
area without knowing at least some of the details of the plan so that we may assess the plan’s
impact on Ms. Flowers’ interests.
4. (a) A decision to place a non-binding question before the voters for their approval or
disapproval ofredevelopment in general.
We cannot answer this question because it is hypothetical and vague. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(D).) Without at least some facts about the wording of the ballot question, we cannot
address this question.


File No. A-97-289
Page 11
4. (b) A decision to place a non-binding question before the voters for their approval or
disapproval of the 1996 proposed redevelopment project area and the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan.
If a public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to a major decision,
he or she is also disqualified from participating in a decision to place the matter on the ballot.
(See, e.g., Benjamin Advice Letter, No. A-86-061.) This is so even if the ballot question is
advisory. (Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-86-127.)
Here, ifMs. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt the
1996 proposed redevelopment plan, including the proposed project boundaries, then she also has
a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place the matter on the ballot, even as an
advisory or nonbinding question. First, although advisory, the outcome of the ballot question
would have a significant impact on the eventual outcome of the matter. You state in your advice
request that it is unlikely that the Council will approve the redevelopment plan if the voters
disapprove the ballot question. Conversely, one may reasonably infer that voter approval ofthe
question would, to at least some degree, increase the chances that the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan would be adopted.
Second, the ultimate decision would remain with the Council. As explained above,
where a series of decisions are so interrelated that they cannot be reasonably segmented into
components, an official who is disqualified as to one ofthe components is disqualified as to the
entire series of decisions. (Cook, supra.) Here, a decision to place the advisory question on the
ballot is too closely related to the Council’s decision to be bifurcated from it: they both deal,
apparently, with the identical issue.
5. A decision to designate a aoverning body, other than the members of the Council, to
act as the Board of the Agency.
We assume that this decision would be an alternative to acting upon the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan, either by adopting, revising, or placing it before the voters. Given that
assumption, the answer to this question depends largely on the answer to question no. 1. If
Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, then she will also have a disqualifying conflict of interest with
regard to a decision to designate a governing body, other than the members of the Council, to act
as the Board of the Agency. A decision to designate another governing body, without acting on
the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, amounts to a “go or no go” decision by the current
Council—it is a decision not to act on the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, which itself
constitutes a governmental decision. (See Regulation 1 8700(b)(5).) Thus, the decision to
designate another governing body, in this factual context, is inextricably interrelated to the
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, and cannot be bifurcated from it.


File No. A-97-289
Page 12
Even if you and Ms. Flowers conclude that she is not disqualified with regard to a
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment pian, she may nonetheless have a conflict of
interest in a decision to designate an alternative governing body for the Agency. We are unable
to advise you on this particular issue because it is hypothetical. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(D).)
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are designed to be applied in the context of a particular
governmental decision. For example, Ms. Flowers might have a conflict of interest with regard
to one potential member of the new governing body because he/she is a source of income to her,
while she would not have a conflict of interest with regard to another individual with whom she
has no financial relationship.
6. A decision to adopt an ordinance deactivatingthe Agency.
Again, we assume that this decision would be an alternative to acting upon the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, either by adopting, revising, or placing it before the voters. Given
that assumption, the answer here is the same as the answer to question no. 5, about a decision to
designate an alternative governing body to act as the Board of the Agency. A decision to
deactivate the Agency is inextricably interrelated to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan because the former decision is tantamount to a decision not to make the
latter decision.
Even if you and Ms. Flowers conclude that she is not disqualified with regard to a
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she may nonetheless have a conflict of
interest in a decision to deactivate the Agency. We do not have enough facts (e.g., about other
projects and other interests ofMs. Flowers which might be affected) to evaluate the conflict of
interest implications of a decision to deactivate the Agency.
E.
The public generally exception.
For a disqualifying conflict of interest to exist, the reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect of a governmental decision on the public official’s financial interest must be
“distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.” (Section 87103.) The material financial
effect on a public official’s fmancial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public
generally if the decision will affect a “significant segment” of the public “in substantially the
same manner” it affects the public official. (Regulation 18703(a)(1),(2).) A “significant
segment” may be comprised of:
•
10 percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the official’s agency;
•
10 percent or more of all property owners, all home owners or all households in the
jurisdiction of the official’s agency;
•
50 percent of all businesses in the jurisdiction so long as the businesses are composed of
more than a single industry, trade, or profession;


File No. A-97-289
Page 13
•
At least 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction; or,
•
The decision will affect a predominant industry, trade, or profession in the official’s
jurisdiction. (Regulations 18703(a)(l)(C) and 18703.2.)
“Substantially the same manner” is defined in Regulation 18703(a)(2):
“(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental decision will
affect the official’s economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will
affect the significant segment identified in subdivision (a)(1) of this regulation.”
In general terms, applying the public generally exception consists of two closely
interrelated steps. First, one must determine whether there is a cognizable “significant segment”
of the public. Second, one must determine whether this significant segment is affected in
“substantially the same manner” as the public official.
1. Real property interests
Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest in a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan arising from her ownership of her residence. (See part IV.D.1.(a), above.)
She is disqualified from making, participating in making, or using her official position to
influence this decision unless the public generally exception applies. (Section 87100.)
Ms. Flowers’ residence is located within 300 feet of the 1996 proposed project
boundaries. The City’s Planning Director has determined that more than 10 percent of the City’s
homeowners are situated in similar geographic proximity to the 1996 proposed project
boundaries. These homeowners would comprise a significant segment ifMs. Flowers concludes
that the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan would indeed affect those
homeowners in substantially the same manner in which it would affect her residence. We must
leave it to Ms. Flowers to make this decision. If she concludes the effect would be substantially
the same, then the public generally exception would apply, and she would not be disqualified as a
result ofher residence. (Note that Ms. Flowers could still be disqualified as a result of other
potential conflicts discussed in this letter.)
2. Source of income interests
As explained above, Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest arising from her father and
aunt as a source of income, and is disqualified unless the public generally exception applies. As
explained above, if the exception is to apply, there must be a significant segment of the public
which is affected in substantially the same manner as is Ms. Flowers’ father as the owner of
commercial buildings and business interests located within the 1996 proposed project


File No. A-97-289
Page 14
boundaries. Such a segment must consist of 50 percent of all businesses (Regulation
18703(a)(1)(B)).
5 In the end, this is essentially a factual determination which Ms. Flowers must
make.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
eneral Counsel
By:
John Tergelli
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JV:jlw
You have stated that all commercially zoned property in the City is included in the proposed project
area, including presumably all commercial buildings such as the ones owned by Ms. Flowers’ father.
When a
governmental decision will affect an entire industry in substantially the same manner as it will effect a public
official’s economic interest, the industry is considered to constitute a significant segment fthat industry is a
“predominant industry” in the jurisdiction or district. (Regulation 18703.2.)
Regulation 18703.2 does not
establish any specific criteria for determining when an industry, trade, or profession is predominant in a given
jurisdiction. We rely on the well-settled interpretation that the “predominant industry” variation of the public
generally exception is to be construed narrowly. (Woods Advice Letter, No. A-94-l64.)
Originally, the term
“predominant” was meant to apply to a situation where a local economy is based on one industry, so that a]most any
public official would have an economic tie to that industry, trade, or profession.
(Ibid.) In Woods, supra, we
advised that the real estate business, while the third most numerous type ofbusiness in the jurisdiction-in-question,
was not the basis of the local economy, and therefore the “predominant industry” variation on the public generally
exception did not apply.
You have not provided sufficient facts with which to determine whether Regulation 18703.2 would apply
to prevent Ms. Flowers’ disqualification in the case of her father. However, given the law stated in the preceding
paragraph, it seems unlikely that the commercial real estate business in your jurisdiction will qualify as a
“predominant industry or trade.”

sections.question:
1. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan?
2. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to select consultants
to gather additional information and to revise the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan?
3. (a) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to designate a new
proposed redevelopment project area boundary without simultaneously adopting a
redevelopment plan for the new area?
3. (b) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt a new
redevelopment plan for a new proposed project area?
4. (a) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place a non
binding question before the voters for their approval or disapproval of redevelopment in general?
(916) 322-5660
4. (b) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place a non
binding question before the voters for their approval or disapproval of the 1996 proposed
redevelopment project area and the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan?
5. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to designate a
governing body, other than the members of the Council, to act as the Board of the Agency?
6. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt an ordinance
deactivating the Agency?
sections.conclusion:
1. Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest in the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan arising from her residence, and from her father and aunt who are sources of
income to her. She may have a conflict of interest arising from the operation of the “personal
effects” rule. Whether she is disqualified as a result ofthese conflicts depends on whether the
public generally exception applies. We cannot make this determination from a distance. We
have provided the relevant legal analysis, and identified the critical questions of fact Ms. Flowers
must answer.
2. The decision to select consultants to gather information and to revise the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan and the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan are too
closely related to be considered separately. Thus, ifMs. Flowers is disqualified with regard to
the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified with regard
to the decision to select consultants to gather information and to revise the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan.
3. (a) and 3. (b). We cannot answer these questions because they are entirely hypothetical.
4. (a) We cannot answer this question because it is entirely hypothetical.
4. (b) If Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to the decision to adopt
the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified from taking part in the decision to
place the matter on the ballot.
5.
The decision to designate an alternative governing body for the Agency and the decision to
adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan are too closely related to be considered separately.
Thus, ifMs. Flowers is disqualified with regard to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified with regard to the decision to designate an
alternative governing body to act as the Board of the Agency.
6. The decision to deactivate the Agency and the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan are too closely related to be considered separately. Thus, if Ms. Flowers is
disqualified with regard to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is
also disqualified with regard to the decision to deactivate the Agency.
sections.facts:
You are the deputy city attorney for the City of Artesia (“City”) and have been authorized
by Ms. Flowers, a member ofthe Artesia City Council (“Council”) to seek formal advice.
In 1995, the City activated a Redevelopment Agency (“Agency”). The Council
designated itself to sit as the governing board (“Board”) of the Agency. In 1996, a proposed
project area (“1996 proposed project area”) was prepared by staff and consultants. The 1996
proposed project area included all commercially zoned property in the City, and excluded all
residentially zoned property in the City.
The 1996 proposed project area and the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan were
presented to the Council in mid-1996. During the Council’s consideration ofthose documents,
the general subject of redevelopment became an issue of significant community debate. Finally,
in August 1996, the Council decided not to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
In order to gain community consensus, the Council then considered whether to seek the
views ofthe voters in Artesia by way of a vote regarding redevelopment. In exploring that
option, the city attorney’s office advised the Council that it has the power to place a non-binding
initiative on the ballot on whether to adopt the 1996 proposed project area, or whether to pursue
redevelopment generally. Although the measure would not legally bind the Council, it appears
unlikely as a political matter that the Council would vote to pursue redevelopment of any sort in
the near future if the electors voted against the concept.
As an alternative to seeking voter approval, and based on the recent election of two new
members to the Council, the Council wants to consider: (i) whether to designate a governing
body, other than the members of the Council, to act as the Board of the Agency; (ii) whether to
select consultants to gather additional information; (iii) whether to revise the 1996 proposed
project area and the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan; (iv) whether to designate a new
hypothetical project area boundary without an implementing plan; (v) whether to adopt a
redevelopment plan for that area; and (vi) whether to adopt an ordinance deactivating the
Agency.
Under the redevelopment law, if the Council decides to move forward with the adoption
of a new preliminary plan, new proceedings would have to occur and the documents would have
to be updated and revised.
Ms. Sally Flowers serves on the Council. Ms. Flowers resides in, and is the sole owner
of, a single-family residence located approximately 300 feet from the boundaries of the 1996
proposed project area. The City’s Planning Director has determined that more than 10 percent of
all homeowners in the City are located within 300 feet from the proposed boundaries of the 1996
project area.
Ms. Flowers has provided weekly cleaning services of the common areas of a building
owned by her father and aunt. For that service, Ms. Flowers has received approximately $25 per
week from her father. Ms. Flowers discontinued these services at the end ofMarch 1997.
During the last twelve months, she has received more than $250 from her father for the service.
Ms. Flowers’ father and aunt own an office building located on a lot within the 1996
proposed project area. Ms. Flowers’ father also owns an interest in a commercially zoned
property located within the 1996 proposed project area. Ms. Flowers’ father operates a trailer
rental yard on the second property.
Ms. Flowers has a roommate. The roommate does not pay Ms. Flowers rent, but does
contribute to the payment of shared household expenses. The contribution to shared expenses
are estimated by Ms. Flowers to have exceeded $250 in the last twelve months.
The roommate has been employed by, and has received more than $250 in income in the
last twelve months from Russell Construction. Russell Construction provides temporary laborers
and contractors to contractors working on projects throughout southern California.
For more than the last twelve months, Ms. Flowers’ roommate has been assigned by
Russell Construction to woi* almost exclusively onjob sites for a contractor called Snyder
Langston. Snyder Langston has not performed any contracting work in the City of Artesia
during the last twelve months and it is not anticipated that it will work on a project in Artesia in
the future.
Snyder Langston pays Russell Construction for the contractors and laborers it provides on
Snyder Langston’s job sites, including Ms. Flowers’ roommate. Russell Construction then pays
Ms. Flowers’ roommate for his services.
When Ms. Flowers’ roommate was not working on ajob site for Snyder Langston, he
worked through Russell Construction for other contractors, none ofwhose job sites were within
the City of Artesia. Thus, Ms. Flowers’ roommate has not worked on job sites in Artesia and it is
not anticipated that he will do so in the future. Ms. Flowers’ roommate recently became
temporarily disabled by a work-related injury and may or may not return to work for Russell
Construction.
Snyder Langston does not own property or operate a business in the City. Russell
Construction leases office space in the City of Artesia in a building that is located within the
boundaries of the originally proposed project area. Russell Construction is a company that is
smaller in financial size than the criteria specified in Section 18702.2(f) for listing on the
NASDAQ.
sections.analysis:
A.
Introduction to the conflict-of-interest provisions.
The purpose of the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that public officials
will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. As a public official,
2 Ms. Flowers will have a disqualifying
conflict of interest with regard to governmental decisions about the redevelopment project in the
City of Artesia if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on
her financial interests which are distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
B.
Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where the public official “make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.”
(Section 87100.)
By voting on any of the potential Council decisions about which you inquire,
Ms. Flowers would be making governmental decisions. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(1).) By taking
part in deliberations and negotiations leading up to a vote, she would be using her official
position to influence the decisions. (Regulation 18700.1(a).)
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are designed to be applied on a decision-by-
decision basis. Each of the various Council decisions about which you have inquired must be
addressed separately.
2 The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to “public officials.” “Public official,” for purposes
of the Act, is defmed to include every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local agency (with
certain exceptions not relevant here). (Section 82048; Regulation 87100.) As a member of the City Council,
Ms. Flowers is a public official.
C.
Identifying financial interests.
1. Introduction.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only tofinancial conflicts. “Financial
interest” is defined in Section 87103. Section 87103 recognizes six kinds of financial interests
for purposes of the Act:
•
A business entity in which the public official has an investment of $1,000 or more;
•
Real property in which the public official has an interest of $1,000 or more;
•
Any source of income which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the
governmental decision;
•
A business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.;
•
A donor of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $290 or more within 12
months prior to the decision;
•
Finally, the public official has a fmancial interest if the governmental decision will have a
“personal effect” on himlher or his/her immediate family, whether positive or negative, of
at least $250 in any 12-month period. (This is known as the “personal effects” rule.)
(Section 87103; Regulation rl 8702.1 (a)(4).)
2. Real property interest
Presuming that Ms. Flowers has invested more than $1,000, she has a financial interest in
her residence. (Section 87103(b).)
3. Source of income interests
(a) Herfather and aunt
Because she has received more than $250 in income in the past twelve months from her
father and aunt, they are a source of income to Ms. Flowers. (Section 87103(c).)
(b) Her roommate
Ms. Flowers’ roommate periodically pays for Ms. Flowers’ dinners and groceries, and
also pays for the utilities. The utilities payment is not a gift because Ms. Flowers provides
consideration of at least equal value to the roommate in the form ofhousing. (Section 82028.)
However, the payments are income to Ms. Flowers, and the roommate is that source of income.
(Section 82030(a); Ball Advice Letter, No. 97-192.) Therefore, the roommate is a source of
income to Ms. Flowers. (Section 87103(c); Ball Advice Letter, supra.)
4. Personal effects rule.
Ms. Flowers must consider whether each ofthe potential decisions about which you have
inquired will have a personal financial effect on her or on her immediate family.
3 (Section
87103, first paragraph.) She may have a financial interest in a given decision if the reasonably
foreseeable impact of the decision results in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities
ofher or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at least $250 in any 12-month period.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)
D. Reasonable foreseeability and materiality.
Whether the financial consequences of a given governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific. A financial effect need
not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will
occur suffices to meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Whether a financial effect is material is determined under various regulations
promulgated by the FPPC, depending upon the nature of the interest and the degree to which it is
involved. (Regulation 18700 et seq.)
It is important to understand that determinations of reasonable foreseeability and
materiality are very fact-dependent, and must be made on a decision-by-decision basis. An effect
which may not be reasonably foreseeable at an early stage of a process may become reasonably
foreseeable as the process unfolds. A “blanket” determination of reasonable foreseeability
cannot be made at any stage of a process or a series of decisions which applies to the entire
process or series of decisions. Therefore, the reasonable foreseeability and materiality of the
potential governmental decisions about which you inquire on Ms. Flowers’ various financial
interests must be considered separately.
For purposes of the Act, “immediate family” means the spouse and dependent children. (Section
82029.)
1. A decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
(a) Realproperty interest
Ms. Flowers has a financial interest in the residence she owns which would be indirectly
involved in a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
4 Whether the financial
effect of a given governmental decision on an indirectly involved real property interest is
material is determined under Regulation 18702.3. That regulation prescribes alternative
materiality rules; which one applies depends primarily upon the geographic proximity ofthe
interest to the area which is the subject ofthe decision.
As to reasonable foreseeability, it is reasonably foreseeable that at least some financial
effect on real property values and business interests located within or near project areas will
result from redevelopment projects. (DeSaulnier Advice Letter, No. A-96-361.) A financial
effect on Ms. Flowers’ real property interest is thus reasonably foreseeable because it is located
near the 1996 proposed project boundaries. The critical issue is thus whether this effect would
be material.
Ms. Flowers’ residence is within 300 feet of the 1996 proposed project area boundaries.
A real property interest within 300 feet ofthe area which is the subject of a decision is deemed to
be materially affected by the decision, unless it can be shown that no financial effect will result.
(Regulation 18702.3(a)(l).) This is a “one penny” rule: if there is any financial effect
whatsoever, Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest. As it is difficult to imagine that there will be
no financial effect whatsoever on Ms. Flowers’ residence, she has a conflict of interest, and is
disqualified unless the “public generally exception” applies. (See part IV.E, below.)
(b) Source ofincome interests
(1) Herfather and aunt
Her father and aunt are a source of income to Ms. Flowers. (See part IV.C, above.) They
are directly involved in a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan because they
own commercial buildings and business entities located within the 1996 proposed project
boundaries. (Regulation 18702.l(b)(2).) A financial effect on Ms. Flowers’ father and aunt and
their real property and business interests is reasonably foreseeable because the property and
businesses are located within the 1996 proposed project area. (DeSaulnier, supra.) Any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a directly involved source of income is deemed to be
material. (Regulation 18702.1 (a)(l).) Therefore, Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest arising
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(3) states the conditions in which a real property interest is considered to be
directly involved in a governmental decision. None of these conditions are true as to Ms. Flowers’ real property
interest in comiection with a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan. Therefore, by default, the
interest would be indirectly involved in the decision.
from her father and aunt as a source of income, and is disqualified unless the public generally
exception applies. (See below.)
(ii) Her roommate
Ms. Flowers’ roommate, who is a source of income to her, is indirectly involved in the
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan. Although he is employed in the
construction trades, and it is likely that a redevelopment decision will have some impact on the
local construction trades, it would be speculative at best to try to gauge the impact ofthe decision
on a particular employee. Therefore, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to adopt
the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan will have a financial effect, much less a material one, on
Ms. Flowers’ roommate. She would not have a conflict of interest based on her roommate as a
source of income.
(c) Personal effects rule
When applying the personal effects rule, financial effects on the value ofreal property
owned by the public official are discounted. (Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4).) Such financial effects
are analyzed independently, as we have done above. Therefore, the effects of the decision on her
residence are discounted when applying the personal effects rule to Ms. Flowers. Discounting
the effects on her residence, Ms. Flowers must decide if a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan will have a “personal effect” on her or her immediate family, whether
positive or negative, of at least $250 in any 12-month period. If so, she has a conflict of interest,
and is disqualified unless the public generally exception applies.
2. A decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan.
The answer to question no. 2 depends largely on the answer to question no. 1: if
Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, then she will also have a disqualifying conflict of interest with
regard to a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan. The two decisions are inextricably interrelated. To state only the
most obvious evidence oftheir interrelatedness, a decision to select consultants to gather
additional information and to revise the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan amounts to a
decision not to adopt the original 1996 proposed redevelopment plan.
Under certain circumstances, a complex decision may be divided into a series of separate
decisions if an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in one of the component-decisions
which is not interdependent upon other components. (Cook Advice Letter, No. 1-91-468.)
However, a series of decisions may be too interrelated to be considered separately.
In the Cook
Advice Letter, supra, we advised that this would be true where a decision on one aspect of a
general plan will substantially affect a decision on another aspect of the plan.
Here, the relationship between a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan
and a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan are inextricably interrelated. Therefore, if you conclude that
Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, then she will also have a disqualifying conflict of interest with
regard to a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan.
Even if you conclude that Ms. Flowers is not disqualified with regard to a decision to
adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she may nonetheless have a conflict of interest
with regard to a decision to select consultants to gather additional information and to revise the
1996 proposed redevelopment plan. We are unable to advise you on this particular issue because
it is hypothetical.
(Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(D).) The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are
designed to be applied in the context of a particular governmental decision. For example,
Ms. Flowers might have a conflict of interest with regard to one consultant because that
consultant is a source of income to her, while she would not have a conflict of interest with
regard to another consultant with whom she has no financial relationship. Similarly, we cannot
advise about revisions to the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan without knowing at least some
of the details of the revision and the expected impact on Ms. Flowers’ interests.
3. (a) A decision to designate anew proposed redevelopment project area boundary
without simultaneously adopting a redevelopment plan for the new area.
3. (b) A decision to adopt a new redevelopment plan for a new proposed project are&
We cannot answer these questions because each is hypothetical. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(D).) The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are designed to be applied in the
context of a particular decision. For example, we cannot advise you about a hypothetical
decision to designate new proposed project boundaries without knowing the geographical
relationship of the boundaries to Ms. Flowers’ real property and other financial interests. We
cannot advise about a decision to adopt a redevelopment plan for this hypothetical new project
area without knowing at least some of the details of the plan so that we may assess the plan’s
impact on Ms. Flowers’ interests.
4. (a) A decision to place a non-binding question before the voters for their approval or
disapproval ofredevelopment in general.
We cannot answer this question because it is hypothetical and vague. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(D).) Without at least some facts about the wording of the ballot question, we cannot
address this question.
4. (b) A decision to place a non-binding question before the voters for their approval or
disapproval of the 1996 proposed redevelopment project area and the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan.
If a public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to a major decision,
he or she is also disqualified from participating in a decision to place the matter on the ballot.
(See, e.g., Benjamin Advice Letter, No. A-86-061.) This is so even if the ballot question is
advisory. (Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-86-127.)
Here, ifMs. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt the
1996 proposed redevelopment plan, including the proposed project boundaries, then she also has
a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place the matter on the ballot, even as an
advisory or nonbinding question. First, although advisory, the outcome of the ballot question
would have a significant impact on the eventual outcome of the matter. You state in your advice
request that it is unlikely that the Council will approve the redevelopment plan if the voters
disapprove the ballot question. Conversely, one may reasonably infer that voter approval ofthe
question would, to at least some degree, increase the chances that the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan would be adopted.
Second, the ultimate decision would remain with the Council. As explained above,
where a series of decisions are so interrelated that they cannot be reasonably segmented into
components, an official who is disqualified as to one ofthe components is disqualified as to the
entire series of decisions. (Cook, supra.) Here, a decision to place the advisory question on the
ballot is too closely related to the Council’s decision to be bifurcated from it: they both deal,
apparently, with the identical issue.
5. A decision to designate a aoverning body, other than the members of the Council, to
act as the Board of the Agency.
We assume that this decision would be an alternative to acting upon the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan, either by adopting, revising, or placing it before the voters. Given that
assumption, the answer to this question depends largely on the answer to question no. 1. If
Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, then she will also have a disqualifying conflict of interest with
regard to a decision to designate a governing body, other than the members of the Council, to act
as the Board of the Agency. A decision to designate another governing body, without acting on
the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, amounts to a “go or no go” decision by the current
Council—it is a decision not to act on the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, which itself
constitutes a governmental decision. (See Regulation 1 8700(b)(5).) Thus, the decision to
designate another governing body, in this factual context, is inextricably interrelated to the
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, and cannot be bifurcated from it.
Even if you and Ms. Flowers conclude that she is not disqualified with regard to a
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment pian, she may nonetheless have a conflict of
interest in a decision to designate an alternative governing body for the Agency. We are unable
to advise you on this particular issue because it is hypothetical. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(D).)
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions are designed to be applied in the context of a particular
governmental decision. For example, Ms. Flowers might have a conflict of interest with regard
to one potential member of the new governing body because he/she is a source of income to her,
while she would not have a conflict of interest with regard to another individual with whom she
has no financial relationship.
6. A decision to adopt an ordinance deactivatingthe Agency.
Again, we assume that this decision would be an alternative to acting upon the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan, either by adopting, revising, or placing it before the voters. Given
that assumption, the answer here is the same as the answer to question no. 5, about a decision to
designate an alternative governing body to act as the Board of the Agency. A decision to
deactivate the Agency is inextricably interrelated to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan because the former decision is tantamount to a decision not to make the
latter decision.
Even if you and Ms. Flowers conclude that she is not disqualified with regard to a
decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she may nonetheless have a conflict of
interest in a decision to deactivate the Agency. We do not have enough facts (e.g., about other
projects and other interests ofMs. Flowers which might be affected) to evaluate the conflict of
interest implications of a decision to deactivate the Agency.
E.
The public generally exception.
For a disqualifying conflict of interest to exist, the reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect of a governmental decision on the public official’s financial interest must be
“distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.” (Section 87103.) The material financial
effect on a public official’s fmancial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public
generally if the decision will affect a “significant segment” of the public “in substantially the
same manner” it affects the public official. (Regulation 18703(a)(1),(2).) A “significant
segment” may be comprised of:
•
10 percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the official’s agency;
•
10 percent or more of all property owners, all home owners or all households in the
jurisdiction of the official’s agency;
•
50 percent of all businesses in the jurisdiction so long as the businesses are composed of
more than a single industry, trade, or profession;
•
At least 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction; or,
•
The decision will affect a predominant industry, trade, or profession in the official’s
jurisdiction. (Regulations 18703(a)(l)(C) and 18703.2.)
“Substantially the same manner” is defined in Regulation 18703(a)(2):
“(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental decision will
affect the official’s economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will
affect the significant segment identified in subdivision (a)(1) of this regulation.”
In general terms, applying the public generally exception consists of two closely
interrelated steps. First, one must determine whether there is a cognizable “significant segment”
of the public. Second, one must determine whether this significant segment is affected in
“substantially the same manner” as the public official.
1. Real property interests
Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest in a decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan arising from her ownership of her residence. (See part IV.D.1.(a), above.)
She is disqualified from making, participating in making, or using her official position to
influence this decision unless the public generally exception applies. (Section 87100.)
Ms. Flowers’ residence is located within 300 feet of the 1996 proposed project
boundaries. The City’s Planning Director has determined that more than 10 percent of the City’s
homeowners are situated in similar geographic proximity to the 1996 proposed project
boundaries. These homeowners would comprise a significant segment ifMs. Flowers concludes
that the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan would indeed affect those
homeowners in substantially the same manner in which it would affect her residence. We must
leave it to Ms. Flowers to make this decision. If she concludes the effect would be substantially
the same, then the public generally exception would apply, and she would not be disqualified as a
result ofher residence. (Note that Ms. Flowers could still be disqualified as a result of other
potential conflicts discussed in this letter.)
2. Source of income interests
As explained above, Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest arising from her father and
aunt as a source of income, and is disqualified unless the public generally exception applies. As
explained above, if the exception is to apply, there must be a significant segment of the public
which is affected in substantially the same manner as is Ms. Flowers’ father as the owner of
commercial buildings and business interests located within the 1996 proposed project
boundaries. Such a segment must consist of 50 percent of all businesses (Regulation
18703(a)(1)(B)).
5 In the end, this is essentially a factual determination which Ms. Flowers must
make.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt the 1996
proposed redevelopment plan?
2. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to select consultants
to gather additional information and to revise the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan?
3. (a) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to designate a new
proposed redevelopment project area boundary without simultaneously adopting a
redevelopment plan for the new area?
3. (b) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt a new
redevelopment plan for a new proposed project area?
4. (a) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place a non
binding question before the voters for their approval or disapproval of redevelopment in general?
(916) 322-5660
4. (b) Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to place a non
binding question before the voters for their approval or disapproval of the 1996 proposed
redevelopment project area and the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan?
5. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to designate a
governing body, other than the members of the Council, to act as the Board of the Agency?
6. Does Ms. Flowers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision to adopt an ordinance
deactivating the Agency?

CONCLUSION: 1. Ms. Flowers has a conflict of interest in the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan arising from her residence, and from her father and aunt who are sources of
income to her. She may have a conflict of interest arising from the operation of the “personal
effects” rule. Whether she is disqualified as a result ofthese conflicts depends on whether the
public generally exception applies. We cannot make this determination from a distance. We
have provided the relevant legal analysis, and identified the critical questions of fact Ms. Flowers
must answer.
2. The decision to select consultants to gather information and to revise the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan and the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan are too
closely related to be considered separately. Thus, ifMs. Flowers is disqualified with regard to
the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified with regard
to the decision to select consultants to gather information and to revise the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan.
3. (a) and 3. (b). We cannot answer these questions because they are entirely hypothetical.
4. (a) We cannot answer this question because it is entirely hypothetical.
4. (b) If Ms. Flowers has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to the decision to adopt
the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified from taking part in the decision to
place the matter on the ballot.
5.
The decision to designate an alternative governing body for the Agency and the decision to
adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan are too closely related to be considered separately.
Thus, ifMs. Flowers is disqualified with regard to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan, she is also disqualified with regard to the decision to designate an
alternative governing body to act as the Board of the Agency.
6. The decision to deactivate the Agency and the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed
redevelopment plan are too closely related to be considered separately. Thus, if Ms. Flowers is
disqualified with regard to the decision to adopt the 1996 proposed redevelopment plan, she is
also disqualified with regard to the decision to deactivate the Agency.
